content:
# Philippians Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 196-197 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_004_page_001.jpg, PHP_004_page_002.jpg

---

1. Therefore, my brothers, beloved and longed for, my joy and crown, stand firm thus in the Lord, beloved.

2. I urge Euodia, and I urge Syntyche, to be of the same mind in the Lord.

3. Yes, I ask you also, true companion, assist these women who labored with me in the gospel, together with Clement and the rest of my fellow workers, whose names are in the book of life.

4. Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice.

5. Let your gentleness be known to all people. The Lord is near.

6. Be anxious about nothing, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God.

7. And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, shall guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.

8. Finally, brothers, whatever things are true, whatever things are honorable, whatever things are just, whatever things are pure, whatever things are lovely, whatever things are of good repute — if there is any virtue and if there is any praise — think on these things.

9. What you have both learned and received and heard and seen in me, practice these things, and the God of peace shall be with you.

10. I rejoiced in the Lord greatly that now at length you have revived your concern for me, in which indeed you were concerned, but you lacked opportunity.

11. Not that I speak according to want, for I have learned to be content in whatever circumstances I am.

12. I know how to be brought low, and I know how to abound. In everything and in all things I have been initiated — both to be filled and to hunger, both to abound and to be in want.

13. I can do all things through the one who strengthens me.

14. Nevertheless, you did well in sharing with me in my affliction.

15. And you yourselves know, Philippians, that in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no church shared with me in the matter of giving and receiving except you alone;

16. for even in Thessalonica you sent to my need once and again.

17. Not that I seek the gift, but I seek the fruit that increases to your account.

18. But I have received all things in full and abound; I have been filled, having received from Epaphroditus the things sent from you, a fragrant offering, an acceptable sacrifice, well-pleasing to God.

19. And my God shall supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.

20. To our God and Father be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

21. Greet every saint in Christ Jesus. The brothers who are with me greet you.

22. All the saints greet you, and especially those of Caesar's household.

23. The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Verse 1 — "brothers" (ἀδελφοί):** Sinaiticus reads ἀδελφοί μου, retained as "my brothers" in the formal register. The fourfold affection in this verse (beloved, longed for, joy, crown) is characteristic of Paul's warmth toward the Philippian congregation.

**Verse 3 — "true companion" (γνήσιε σύζυγε):** The identity of this addressee remains debated. The term σύζυγε could be a proper name (Syzygus) or a descriptive epithet ("yokefellow," "companion"). I have rendered it "true companion" to preserve the ambiguity present in the Greek.

**Verse 5 — "gentleness" (ἐπιεικές):** Sinaiticus reads τὸ ἐπιεικὲς ὑμῶν. This term carries connotations of reasonableness, forbearance, and magnanimity — broader than mere "gentleness," but no single English word captures the full range. "Gentleness" is adopted as the most natural formal equivalent.

**Verse 7 — "shall guard" (φρουρήσει):** The future indicative is preserved as "shall guard" rather than the softer "will guard," maintaining the promissory and declarative force of Paul's assurance. This is a military metaphor — God's peace standing sentinel over the believer's inner life.

**Verse 8 — Virtue catalogue:** The six adjectives (true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, of good repute) followed by the conditional clauses represent a rhetorical structure drawing on both Jewish wisdom and Greco-Roman moral vocabulary. Sinaiticus preserves the standard text here without significant variation.

**Verse 12 — "initiated" (μεμύημαι):** This term derives from mystery-religion vocabulary (μυέω), meaning to be instructed in secrets or initiated. Paul repurposes it: his "initiation" is into the discipline of contentment through all circumstances. "Initiated" preserves this rich connotation better than the flatter "learned the secret."

**Verse 13 — "through the one who strengthens me":** Sinaiticus reads τῷ ἐνδυναμοῦντί με without the explicit name "Christ," which appears in later manuscripts. The formal translation follows Sinaiticus in leaving the strengthening agent identified only as "the one who strengthens me."

**Verse 18 — "fragrant offering" (ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας):** This phrase echoes Old Testament sacrificial language (cf. Genesis 8:21 LXX; Exodus 29:18), drawing a direct line between the Philippians' financial gift and acceptable worship before God.

**Verse 23 — "your spirit" (τοῦ πνεύματος ὑμῶν):** Sinaiticus reads the closing benediction with "your spirit" (plural possessive, singular πνεύματος), addressing the congregation collectively. Some manuscripts add further greetings; Sinaiticus concludes simply with "Amen."

**General note on Sinaiticus readings:** The Philippians 4 text in Sinaiticus is largely consistent with the critical text, with the notable omission of "Christ" in verse 13 being the most significant variant. The manuscript shows clear, continuous scriptio continua across the columns on pages 196-197, with Philippians concluding partway through the final column of page 197 before Colossians begins.

— LOGOS
